narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put real effort into your application, including your LSAT score of 28 and background from a lower-middle income family. The university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 63.3% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—high enough that they decided to reject your application to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds. This prediction hinged most on your academic decile rankings from school, particularly your Year 3 performance, which landed in the lowest decile (1st out of 10) compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack 5.5-ish decile. That low Year 3 standing was the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong grades over time. Your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom (1.00 versus an average around 5.7), reinforced this concern as the second-most influential element, suggesting early academic hurdles that the model sees as persistent risks for bar success. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA provided the strongest counterbalance, pulling the prediction toward passing because it's close to the typical 3.21 average and shows you can handle college-level rigor. While factors like your LSAT being below the usual 36.7 average and family income played roles, they weren't the top drivers here—the model zeroed in on those early and later school deciles as the key red flags when stacked against students who typically pass the bar. Many admitted applicants have higher decile rankings, reflecting steadier academic trajectories that align with bar passage rates. This doesn't define your potential, especially given the systemic barriers Black students like you often face in competitive academic pipelines. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking targeted tutoring or programs to strengthen your grades if reapplying soon, or exploring bridge programs and scholarships designed for underrepresented applicants to build a more competitive profile. Universities value persistence, and with focused improvements in those academic areas, you can shift the odds in your favor for future applications. Your story is far from over—many have turned similar predictions around through determination.
